name: Media & Society
description: An elective course examining the complex relationships between mass media and society, exploring how media shapes and reflects culture, politics, identity, and democracy.

modules.title: Media's Role in Society
modules.objectives: Explain the major social functions of mass media; evaluate how media simultaneously reflects and shapes social values and norms.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 1.1: Mass Communication — A Critical Approach
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Mass Media and Its Social Role
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Media Shapes Society and Public Opinion
modules.practiceNote: List and evaluate five specific functions media serves in your own community
modules.assignment: Identify a major social event (election, natural disaster, cultural celebration) from the past year. Write a 350-word analysis explaining how at least three different media (social media, television news, and a newspaper) each played a different social role during the event — information dissemination, community building, agenda-setting, or cultural reflection.

modules.title: Media Literacy
modules.objectives: Apply media literacy frameworks to critically evaluate media messages; identify ownership, purpose, and audience in any media text.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 1.4: Media Literacy
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Spot Fake News and Evaluate Media Sources
modules.practiceNote: Apply the five key media literacy questions to three different media texts
modules.assignment: Using the five core media literacy questions (who created this? for what purpose? what values/views are included/excluded? how might others interpret it differently? what techniques are used?), analyze three different media texts: a news article, an advertisement, and a social media post. Write a 400-word report summarizing your findings.

modules.title: Agenda-Setting & Framing
modules.objectives: Explain agenda-setting and framing theories; analyze how media coverage influences which issues the public considers important and how they interpret them.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 2.1: Mass Media and Its Messages
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Agenda-Setting in Mass Media
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Media Framing and Agenda-Setting Explained
modules.practiceNote: Compare front-page coverage of three newspapers on the same day and analyze which issues are framed as most important
modules.assignment: Select a current social or political issue. Compare how it is framed by two news outlets with different audiences or political leanings (e.g., Fox News vs. MSNBC, or a conservative vs. liberal newspaper). Write a 400-word analysis identifying specific framing differences in headlines, word choice, image selection, and which sources are quoted.

modules.title: Cultivation Theory
modules.objectives: Explain George Gerbner's Cultivation Theory and the mean world syndrome; evaluate empirical evidence for media's long-term effects on worldview.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 2.2: How the Media Shapes Culture
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Cultivation Theory and the Mean World Syndrome
modules.practiceNote: Survey classmates on crime rate perceptions and compare heavy vs. light TV viewers' estimates
modules.assignment: Find current statistics on violent crime rates in the United States and compare them to how crime is portrayed in a current television crime drama (CSI, Law & Order, etc.). Write a 350-word analysis applying Cultivation Theory: in what specific ways does the TV show's portrayal differ from statistical reality, and what 'mean world' beliefs might heavy viewers develop?

modules.title: Social Learning from Media
modules.objectives: Apply Albert Bandura's Social Learning Theory to media effects; analyze how observational learning from media influences attitudes and behaviors.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 2.3: The Relationship Between Media and Culture
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bandura's Bobo Doll Experiment and Media Effects
modules.practiceNote: Apply Social Learning Theory to analyze a media role model you have observed
modules.assignment: Identify a positive role model you have encountered through media (athlete, scientist, activist, fictional character) who influenced your attitudes or aspirations. Write a 350-word personal analysis applying Bandura's Social Learning Theory: what behaviors or values did you observe, what made this media figure motivating and worth modeling, and how did media exposure shape this learning?

modules.title: Media & Political Life
modules.objectives: Analyze how media coverage shapes political knowledge, participation, and candidate image; evaluate the role of media in democratic discourse.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 14.1: Government Regulations and the Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Media and Political Life
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Media Influences Political Opinions
modules.practiceNote: Analyze the media coverage of a political candidate using agenda-setting and framing concepts
modules.assignment: Track a politician or political issue in the news for three days. Record which outlet covers it, how much time/space is given, what tone is used (positive, neutral, negative), and whose perspectives are quoted. Write a 400-word analysis evaluating whether the coverage is balanced and how it might influence public perception of the issue or politician.

modules.title: Media & Consumer Culture
modules.objectives: Analyze how advertising and media have created and sustained consumer culture; evaluate the relationship between media content, brand messaging, and consumerism.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 12.1: History of Advertising
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Advertising and Consumer Behavior
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Advertising Creates Consumer Culture
modules.practiceNote: Count and categorize every advertisement you encounter in one hour of normal media use
modules.assignment: Spend one hour normally browsing media (social media, streaming, news sites) and count every advertisement you are exposed to. Categorize them by type of appeal (emotional, status-based, fear-based, aspirational). Write a 350-word reflection on what this exercise reveals about the saturation of advertising in daily life and how consumer culture is maintained through media.

modules.title: Gender in Media
modules.objectives: Analyze how gender is constructed and represented in mainstream media; evaluate the social consequences of gender stereotypes and idealized images in advertising and entertainment.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 9.1: The History of Women in Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Gender Representation in Media
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Media and Gender Stereotypes: How Images Shape Identity
modules.practiceNote: Apply the Bechdel Test to five films and analyze the results
modules.assignment: Select three advertisements targeting different genders (e.g., a toy ad, a cleaning product ad, a car ad). Write a 400-word analysis examining: what gender norms each advertisement reinforces or challenges, what 'ideal' male or female behavior the ad implies, and how such portrayals may influence young people's sense of identity and expectations.

modules.title: Race & Ethnicity in Media
modules.objectives: Analyze patterns of racial and ethnic representation in mainstream US media; evaluate how media both reflects and perpetuates racial stereotypes and inequities.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 10.1: Race and Media Representations
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Race and Ethnicity in Media Representation
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Racial Stereotypes in Hollywood and Mass Media
modules.practiceNote: Conduct a content analysis of a one-hour primetime TV program for racial representation
modules.assignment: Watch the first episode of a popular TV series or a major film and conduct a racial representation analysis. Document the race/ethnicity of each named character and their primary role (hero, villain, supporting, comic relief). Write a 400-word analysis comparing your findings to US Census demographic data and discussing the potential social impact of any patterns you observe.

modules.title: Media & Youth
modules.objectives: Evaluate research on media effects on youth including academic performance, mental health, body image, and social development; develop evidence-based positions on screen time and digital media use.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 2.3: The Relationship Between Media and Culture
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Social Media and Youth Mental Health
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How Social Media Affects Teen Mental Health (Research Overview)
modules.practiceNote: Track and reflect on your own media use patterns for 48 hours using a screen time log
modules.assignment: Track your own media use for 48 hours using your device's built-in screen time tracker (or a paper log). Write a 400-word critical reflection: analyze which apps or platforms dominate your time, evaluate whether your media use supports or undermines your wellbeing and academics, and propose two specific, realistic changes you could make to your media habits.

modules.title: Privacy & Surveillance
modules.objectives: Explain how digital media technologies enable data collection and surveillance; evaluate the tension between privacy rights and national security or commercial interests.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 11.3: Issues and Trends in the Digital Age
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Internet Privacy and Data Security
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Era of Surveillance Capitalism Explained
modules.practiceNote: Review the privacy settings and data collection disclosures of three apps you use regularly
modules.assignment: Read the privacy policy of one popular social media platform or smartphone app (summarize the key points since they are often very long). Write a 400-word analysis: what personal data does the company collect, how do they use it, what rights do you have over your data, and do you believe this level of data collection is ethically acceptable? Justify your position.

modules.title: Fake News & Misinformation
modules.objectives: Distinguish misinformation, disinformation, and propaganda; analyze the social and political consequences of misinformation and evaluate strategies to counter it.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 1.4: Media Literacy
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Evaluating News and Identifying Misinformation
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Why Fake News Spreads and How to Stop It
modules.practiceNote: Use a fact-checking website (PolitiFact, FactCheck.org, Snopes) to evaluate three viral social media claims
modules.assignment: Find a viral claim or story that has been marked as misinformation or false by a reputable fact-checker. Write a 400-word case study: what was the false claim, how did it spread, what made it believable to many people, what harm did it cause, and what could be done at the individual, platform, and societal level to prevent similar misinformation from spreading.

modules.title: Media & Democracy
modules.objectives: Evaluate the press's watchdog role in democracy; analyze threats to press freedom including censorship, corporate consolidation, and political pressure.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 14.1: Government Regulations and the Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — First Amendment and Press Freedom
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Why a Free Press Matters for Democracy
modules.practiceNote: Research the Reporters Without Borders Press Freedom Index and compare the US ranking to three other democracies
modules.assignment: Research the current state of press freedom in the United States using the Reporters Without Borders Press Freedom Index and at least one other credible source. Write a 400-word argument essay evaluating: How strong is press freedom in the US? What are the most significant current threats to press freedom? What is at stake for democracy if press freedom weakens?

modules.title: Future of Media
modules.objectives: Evaluate emerging trends in media technology including AI-generated content, virtual reality, and immersive media; assess their potential social consequences.
modules.readingNote: Understanding Media and Culture (UMN OER) — Ch 16.1: Globalization and the Media
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The Future of the Internet and Digital Media
modules.video2Note: YouTube — AI and the Future of News Media
modules.practiceNote: Research one emerging media technology and evaluate two potential positive and two potential negative societal effects
modules.assignment: Research one emerging media technology (AI-generated news, deepfakes, virtual reality social spaces, or the metaverse). Write a 400-word forward-looking analysis: describe what the technology does and its current state of development, identify two specific potential benefits for society, identify two specific potential harms, and argue whether you believe the technology should be regulated and how.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a social function of mass media?
options: Generating profit for shareholders
Socialization — transmitting cultural norms and values across society
Guaranteeing access to all information regardless of privacy
Replacing all face-to-face communication
quizQuestions.explanation: One of mass media's key social functions is socialization — transmitting shared cultural norms, values, and social expectations across generations and communities.

quizQuestions.question: The idea that media simultaneously reflects AND shapes society refers to:
options: The gatekeeper effect
The two-way relationship between media and culture
The direct effects model of media influence
The propaganda model of mass communication
quizQuestions.explanation: Media reflects existing social values while also shaping new norms and attitudes — a dynamic, bidirectional relationship between media systems and society.

quizQuestions.question: The surveillance function of media refers to media's role in:
options: Monitoring citizens on behalf of governments
Gathering and disseminating information about the environment and world events
Selling user data to advertising companies
Entertaining audiences to distract from political issues
quizQuestions.explanation: The surveillance function (Laswell) describes media as a watchful system that monitors the environment and alerts the public to important developments.

quizQuestions.question: Which theorist proposed that mass media performs the functions of surveillance, correlation, and cultural transmission?
options: Marshall McLuhan
Harold Lasswell
George Gerbner
Paul Lazarsfeld
quizQuestions.explanation: Harold Lasswell (1948) identified three key functions of mass media: surveillance of the environment, correlation (interpreting events), and cultural transmission.

quizQuestions.question: Media's 'correlation' function refers to its role in:
options: Statistically measuring audience demographics
Interpreting and giving meaning to information about the environment
Connecting advertisers with targeted consumer audiences
Creating social networks among community members
quizQuestions.explanation: The correlation function involves media interpreting and making sense of events — editorials, analysis, and commentary that help audiences understand the significance of information.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the best example of applying media literacy skills?
options: Accepting a news story as true because it appears on a well-known website
Checking the original source of a claim before sharing it
Sharing emotional stories quickly to help spread important information
Trusting news stories more when they have many comments
quizQuestions.explanation: Media literacy involves critical evaluation of sources and claims, including tracing information to original, credible sources before accepting or sharing it.

quizQuestions.question: The question 'who created this message?' in media literacy is important because:
options: Knowing the creator's identity prevents copyright violations
Every message is constructed by someone with a particular perspective, purpose, or interest
Creator identity determines whether the content is legal
Messages created by unknown authors are always false
quizQuestions.explanation: Identifying who created a message helps evaluate potential biases, interests, and perspectives that shaped how the message was constructed.

quizQuestions.question: In media literacy, 'point of view' analysis asks:
options: From whose perspective is this story told, and whose voices are missing?
At what camera angle was the image taken?
What political party produced this content?
Is this content written in first or third person?
quizQuestions.explanation: Point of view analysis examines whose perspectives are represented in media content and, critically, whose perspectives are absent or marginalized.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a marker of a credible news source?
options: High emotional impact and dramatic headlines
Clear authorship, sourcing, corrections policy, and editorial standards
The largest number of social media followers
Fastest publication speed among competing outlets
quizQuestions.explanation: Credible news sources identify their journalists, cite their sources, disclose how stories are reported, maintain corrections policies, and adhere to journalistic ethical standards.

quizQuestions.question: Confirmation bias in media consumption refers to the tendency to:
options: Trust media sources that have a long publication history
Seek out and believe information that confirms existing beliefs while dismissing contradictory evidence
Verify every piece of information before sharing it online
Only consume news from government-approved sources
quizQuestions.explanation: Confirmation bias is the cognitive tendency to favor information that confirms pre-existing beliefs and to discount or avoid information that challenges them.

quizQuestions.question: Agenda-setting theory was developed through research on:
options: The effect of television violence on children's behavior
The relationship between what media covers and what the public thinks is important
How advertising shapes consumer purchasing decisions
The ways social media algorithms create echo chambers
quizQuestions.explanation: McCombs and Shaw developed agenda-setting theory through their Chapel Hill study, finding that media emphasis on certain issues corresponded with what voters considered the most important issues.

quizQuestions.question: Second-level agenda setting (framing) proposes that media not only tells us WHAT to think about, but also:
options: When to think about particular issues
HOW to think about issues — what attributes to associate with them
WHETHER the issue is real or manufactured
WHERE to find more information about issues
quizQuestions.explanation: Second-level agenda setting (framing) goes beyond issue salience to argue that media also influences HOW audiences think about issues by emphasizing certain attributes or angles.

quizQuestions.question: Framing an immigration story as an 'economic opportunity' versus a 'national security crisis' is an example of:
options: Agenda-setting at the first level
Media framing shaping interpretation of the same event
Objective reporting of two different aspects of immigration
Both frames being equally accurate and unbiased
quizQuestions.explanation: These two frames emphasize very different aspects of the same issue — economic benefits vs. security threats — shaping audience interpretation and emotional response differently.

quizQuestions.question: Media framing effects are most powerful when:
options: Audiences have strong prior knowledge and opinions on an issue
Audiences have little prior knowledge and rely on media to interpret new issues
Media coverage is brief and limited to a single outlet
The issue is only covered by local rather than national media
quizQuestions.explanation: Framing effects are strongest for issues audiences know little about, when they rely on media frames to make sense of events — a phenomenon called 'media dependency.'

quizQuestions.question: A newspaper that dedicates its front page to celebrity gossip over international news is exercising which media power?
options: Framing — shaping how stories are interpreted
Gatekeeping and agenda-setting — determining what is considered newsworthy
Cultivation — influencing long-term worldview
Social learning — providing behavioral models
quizQuestions.explanation: By choosing what to place on the front page, the newspaper exercises gatekeeping and agenda-setting power — signaling what issues are most important while silencing others.

quizQuestions.question: George Gerbner developed Cultivation Theory through research analyzing:
options: The economic structure of the film industry
The content of television programs compared to audience worldview beliefs
The purchasing behavior of heavy radio listeners
Social media algorithms' effects on political opinions
quizQuestions.explanation: Gerbner's research at the Annenberg School analyzed the content of television programming and compared it to what heavy vs. light TV viewers believed about the real world.

quizQuestions.question: According to Cultivation Theory, heavy viewers of crime dramas are MOST likely to:
options: Become more empathetic toward crime victims
Overestimate the amount of crime in society
Develop a desire to pursue a career in law enforcement
Believe that the justice system effectively punishes criminals
quizQuestions.explanation: Cultivation Theory predicts that heavy exposure to crime-heavy TV content leads viewers to perceive the world as more crime-ridden than reality — the 'mean world syndrome.'

quizQuestions.question: The difference between 'first-order' and 'second-order' cultivation effects is:
options: First-order effects are immediate; second-order effects take years to develop
First-order effects involve specific facts (e.g., crime rates); second-order effects involve broader worldview beliefs
First-order effects affect children; second-order effects affect adults
First-order effects are caused by TV; second-order effects are caused by social media
quizQuestions.explanation: First-order cultivation effects are specific beliefs (e.g., overestimating crime rates); second-order effects are broader worldview attitudes (e.g., general distrust, fear of strangers).

quizQuestions.question: Cultivation Theory is most applicable to which type of media consumption?
options: Brief, occasional exposure to diverse media types
Long-term, heavy consumption of a single medium (especially television)
Interactive social media use involving real-time discussion
News consumption through multiple credible sources
quizQuestions.explanation: Cultivation effects develop through long-term, repetitive exposure to consistent media messages — most studied in the context of heavy television viewing over extended periods.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following would be the strongest evidence AGAINST Cultivation Theory?
options: A heavy TV viewer who believes the world is dangerous
A study showing heavy TV viewers have the same worldview beliefs as light viewers
A crime drama that shows crime rates accurately matching statistics
An audience member who changes their TV viewing habits after learning about cultivation effects
quizQuestions.explanation: If heavy and light viewers held identical worldview beliefs, this would undermine cultivation theory's core claim that heavy TV viewing shapes perception of reality differently than light viewing.

quizQuestions.question: Albert Bandura's Social Learning Theory proposes that people learn behaviors by:
options: Only experiencing rewards and punishments directly
Observing and modeling the behavior of others
Receiving formal instruction from authority figures
Being genetically predisposed to certain behaviors
quizQuestions.explanation: Social Learning Theory holds that people learn through observation of others' behavior, noting consequences, and modeling behavior accordingly — without requiring direct personal experience.

quizQuestions.question: For social learning from media to occur, which of the following is NOT required?
options: Attention — noticing the modeled behavior
Retention — remembering the behavior
Physical practice — personally performing the behavior to a high standard
Motivation — having a reason to imitate the behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Bandura's social learning model requires attention, retention, reproduction (ability to perform), and motivation. However, perfect physical practice is not a prerequisite for learning through observation.

quizQuestions.question: The Bobo Doll experiment demonstrated that children:
options: Became afraid of aggressive behavior after watching it on TV
Imitated aggressive behavior they observed adults perform toward an inflatable doll
Preferred peaceful play to aggressive play regardless of what they observed
Could not distinguish between real and filmed violence
quizQuestions.explanation: In Bandura's Bobo Doll experiments, children who observed adults hitting an inflatable doll were significantly more likely to imitate those aggressive behaviors than children who did not observe them.

quizQuestions.question: Media role models are most influential through social learning when they:
options: Appear in only one brief media exposure
Are attractive, relatable, and seen to be rewarded for their behaviors
Portray negative behaviors that audiences are warned against
Appear only in fictional rather than reality-based programming
quizQuestions.explanation: Social learning theory predicts that models who are attractive, similar to the observer, and seen to be rewarded for their behavior are most likely to be imitated.

quizQuestions.question: Self-efficacy in Bandura's theory refers to:
options: The belief that one can successfully perform a specific behavior
The ability to learn new behaviors independently without observation
The automatic imitation of any behavior seen in media
A genetic predisposition to be influenced by media models
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-efficacy is a person's belief in their capacity to execute a behavior successfully. Media models can boost self-efficacy by showing viewers that a behavior is achievable.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'horse race' journalism in election coverage refers to:
options: Coverage of political events involving actual horse races as metaphors
Media focus on poll standings and candidate competition rather than policy substance
The tradition of presidential candidates riding horses in campaign parades
Investigative reporting on campaign finance and corruption
quizQuestions.explanation: Horse race journalism prioritizes election 'standings' (poll numbers, momentum, who is winning) over substantive coverage of candidates' policy positions and qualifications.

quizQuestions.question: The 'rally around the flag' phenomenon in media and politics occurs when:
options: Media encourages patriotic behavior during national celebrations
Public approval of a president surges during national crises as media coverage shifts to supporting leadership
News organizations stop critical coverage of government during wartime
Politicians use flag imagery in campaign advertising
quizQuestions.explanation: Rally around the flag describes the short-term boost in presidential approval and reduced critical media coverage that typically follows national crises such as terrorist attacks or military actions.

quizQuestions.question: Media's agenda-setting role in elections means that:
options: Media directly tells voters which candidate to support
The issues media emphasizes tend to become the issues voters consider most important
Politicians' actual policies are always the most covered election topics
Social media has replaced traditional media in setting the political agenda
quizQuestions.explanation: In elections, media coverage patterns shape which policy issues voters prioritize — issues receiving heavy coverage become more salient in voters' minds when evaluating candidates.

quizQuestions.question: Citizens who primarily get their political news from social media are MOST at risk of:
options: Being exposed to too many different political perspectives
Living in filter bubbles with limited exposure to diverse political views
Becoming more politically informed than print newspaper readers
Receiving excessive accurate, fact-checked political information
quizQuestions.explanation: Social media algorithms optimize for engagement, which often means showing users content that reinforces existing political beliefs, creating filter bubbles that limit exposure to diverse perspectives.

quizQuestions.question: The 'hypodermic needle' (or 'magic bullet') model of media effects proposed that:
options: Media effects are gradual and cumulative over long periods
Media messages directly and powerfully shape all audience attitudes and behaviors uniformly
Audiences actively resist media messages that conflict with existing beliefs
Media effects vary dramatically based on individual audience characteristics
quizQuestions.explanation: The hypodermic needle model (early 20th century) portrayed media as directly 'injecting' messages into passive audiences, producing uniform, powerful effects — a view now considered overly simplistic.

quizQuestions.question: Consumer culture refers to a social system in which:
options: Consumers have legal protections from corporate exploitation
Identity, social status, and meaning are primarily constructed through purchasing and owning goods
Citizens are educated to resist advertising manipulation
The government controls what goods are produced and sold
quizQuestions.explanation: Consumer culture is a social and economic system in which identity, status, and fulfillment are primarily achieved through acquiring and displaying consumer goods — heavily maintained by advertising.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of 'manufactured demand' in advertising refers to:
options: Using factory production to reduce the cost of advertised goods
Creating desire for products that consumers did not previously feel they needed
Measuring actual consumer demand through market research
Manufacturing advertising materials in foreign countries to reduce costs
quizQuestions.explanation: Advertising often creates or manufactures demand — using emotional appeals, social comparison, and aspirational imagery to make people feel they need products they were unaware they wanted.

quizQuestions.question: Product placement in entertainment media involves:
options: Organizing store shelves to make products more visible to shoppers
Embedding branded products within entertainment content (TV shows, films) as a form of advertising
Selecting the best location to build retail stores
Testing products before deciding where to sell them
quizQuestions.explanation: Product placement is the paid integration of branded products into entertainment content, designed to create brand associations and desire without the audience being fully aware they are watching an advertisement.

quizQuestions.question: Which theoretical perspective would argue that advertising in mass media primarily serves the economic interests of corporations at the expense of consumers?
options: Uses and Gratifications Theory
Critical/Marxist Theory
Social Learning Theory
Agenda-Setting Theory
quizQuestions.explanation: Critical and Marxist media theories argue that corporate-owned media and advertising systems serve the interests of the capitalist class, manufacturing consent and consumption that benefits corporations.

quizQuestions.question: Lifestyle advertising connects products to:
options: Specific functional features and technical specifications
Aspirational identities, values, and social groups rather than product features
Factual comparisons with competing products
Environmental and sustainability benefits
quizQuestions.explanation: Lifestyle advertising sells not just a product but a desired identity, social status, or way of life — linking the brand to aspirational images of happiness, success, or belonging.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'hegemonic masculinity' in media studies refers to:
options: The statistical dominance of male characters in media
The dominant cultural ideal of masculinity promoted in mainstream media as the standard men should meet
Legal discrimination against men in the entertainment industry
The use of male actors to portray historically male roles
quizQuestions.explanation: Hegemonic masculinity describes the dominant, idealized form of masculinity (strong, emotionally restrained, powerful, heterosexual) that mainstream media promotes and that men are expected to aspire to.

quizQuestions.question: Research on media and body image has found that exposure to idealized body images in media:
options: Has no measurable effect on audience body satisfaction
Is associated with increased body dissatisfaction, particularly among women and girls
Consistently motivates healthy diet and exercise behaviors
Only affects people who already have body image concerns
quizQuestions.explanation: Extensive research links exposure to idealized body images in advertising and entertainment to decreased body satisfaction, disordered eating behaviors, and lower self-esteem.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'femvertising' refers to advertising that:
options: Is produced by and for female-owned businesses
Uses feminist messages and female empowerment to sell products
Targets exclusively female consumers with traditional gender imagery
Shows women in traditional domestic roles to appeal to homemakers
quizQuestions.explanation: Femvertising is a marketing strategy that uses feminist themes, empowerment messaging, and positive female representation to build brand image and appeal to female consumers.

quizQuestions.question: The 'glass ceiling' metaphor in the context of media and gender refers to:
options: Transparent walls in modern office buildings
Invisible barriers that limit women's advancement to top positions in media industries
The reflective quality of television screens
Regulations restricting female ownership of media companies
quizQuestions.explanation: The glass ceiling describes the invisible but real barriers that prevent women (and other underrepresented groups) from advancing to top leadership positions in industries including media.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best represents a progressive change in gender representation in recent media?
options: Eliminating female characters from action films
Featuring complex, multi-dimensional female characters as protagonists in major films
Increasing the number of female characters in supporting roles
Reducing the overall number of female characters to reflect historical periods accurately
quizQuestions.explanation: Progress in gender representation involves moving from stereotypical, objectified, or passive female characters toward complex, active, multi-dimensional female protagonists with their own goals and agency.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a persistent pattern in racial representation found in US mainstream media research?
options: Minority groups are significantly overrepresented relative to their population share
Minority groups are often underrepresented or represented in narrow stereotypical roles
Racial diversity in media accurately mirrors US Census demographic data
Media representation of race has no measurable effect on audience attitudes
quizQuestions.explanation: Research consistently shows that racial and ethnic minorities are often underrepresented in US media or confined to stereotypical roles (criminal, athlete, comic relief) that don't reflect the full range of their experiences.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'whitewashing' in film and media refers to:
options: Using bright lighting to make film sets look clean
Casting white actors to play characters who are people of color in the original source material
Editing films to remove racially offensive content for modern audiences
The historical process of excluding people of color from certain professions
quizQuestions.explanation: Whitewashing is the practice of casting White actors in roles that were written as or are historically people of color, erasing non-White representation from mainstream media.

quizQuestions.question: The 'magical Negro' trope in film refers to:
options: Black superheroes who use magical powers to fight crime
A Black supporting character whose primary function is to help a White protagonist succeed, with minimal story of their own
African American spiritual or religious figures in historical films
Black characters who use intelligence and education to overcome racial barriers
quizQuestions.explanation: The 'magical negro' trope (named by Spike Lee) describes a Black character who exists primarily to guide or assist a White main character, denying them a story of their own.

quizQuestions.question: Diverse representation in media matters because it:
options: Is required by federal equal opportunity employment regulations
Shapes what audiences believe is 'normal,' valuable, or possible for different groups
Automatically improves a film's commercial success at the box office
Ensures all content is free from any potentially offensive elements
quizQuestions.explanation: Research shows that who appears in media — and how they are portrayed — shapes social norms, aspirations, stereotypes, and perceptions of different racial and ethnic groups.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of 'symbolic annihilation,' developed by Gaye Tuchman, refers to the media practice of:
options: Using violent imagery to symbolize cultural conflict in film
Rendering certain groups invisible through underrepresentation or trivializing their portrayal
Censoring any media content that depicts minority experiences
Replacing real events with symbolic or allegorical representations
quizQuestions.explanation: Symbolic annihilation describes how media renders women and minority groups invisible through absence, condemnation, or trivialization — as if they do not exist or do not matter.

quizQuestions.question: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends limiting screen time for children primarily because research suggests excessive screen time is associated with:
options: Increased political awareness and engagement
Disrupted sleep, reduced physical activity, and potential developmental effects
Improved academic performance through educational content
Stronger social skills through online peer interaction
quizQuestions.explanation: Research links excessive screen time in children to disrupted sleep patterns, reduced physical activity, and potential negative effects on cognitive and social development.

quizQuestions.question: Research on social media use and adolescent mental health has most consistently found:
options: Social media use has no measurable effect on mental health
Heavy social media use is associated with increased depression and anxiety, especially among girls
Social media use consistently improves mental health through social connection
The effects of social media are identical for all adolescents regardless of how they use it
quizQuestions.explanation: Longitudinal research (Twenge, Haidt, et al.) has found associations between heavy social media use and increased depression and anxiety, particularly among adolescent girls.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of 'FOMO' (Fear of Missing Out) describes:
options: Children's fear of losing access to educational technology
Anxiety created by social media exposure to peers' activities and social lives
A cognitive bias caused by excessive news consumption
Parental concern about children using media unsupervised
quizQuestions.explanation: FOMO describes anxiety triggered by social media exposure to curated images of peers having fun, attending events, or forming connections — creating feelings of social exclusion.

quizQuestions.question: Media multitasking (using multiple media simultaneously) most commonly affects:
options: Positively increasing productivity and learning
Negatively affecting concentration, depth of processing, and academic performance
Improving social communication skills among teenagers
Having no measurable effect on cognitive performance
quizQuestions.explanation: Research consistently finds that media multitasking (e.g., homework while scrolling social media) reduces depth of learning, increases errors, and impairs academic performance.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of proactive digital citizenship for young media users?
options: Sharing personal information freely to build a large following
Verifying information before sharing, protecting privacy, and treating others respectfully online
Only consuming media from sources recommended by algorithms
Avoiding all critical analysis of media to prevent bias
quizQuestions.explanation: Proactive digital citizenship involves thoughtful, responsible, and ethical participation in online spaces — including verification practices, privacy protection, and respectful interaction.

quizQuestions.question: Surveillance capitalism, as described by Shoshana Zuboff, refers to:
options: Government agencies using media to monitor citizens for security purposes
An economic system where companies extract and sell user behavioral data as a commodity
The use of security cameras in public spaces to prevent crime
A business model where consumers pay to monitor their own data usage
quizQuestions.explanation: Zuboff's surveillance capitalism describes how tech companies (Google, Facebook) harvest massive behavioral data from users and sell predictions about behavior to advertisers.

quizQuestions.question: The Fourth Amendment to the US Constitution protects citizens from:
options: Government restrictions on freedom of speech
Unreasonable searches and seizures of their persons and property
Corporations collecting data without user consent
Media companies publishing private information
quizQuestions.explanation: The Fourth Amendment protects citizens from unreasonable government search and seizure; its application to digital privacy (email, location data) is an evolving area of constitutional law.

quizQuestions.question: A website's privacy policy typically describes:
options: The website's political stance on government surveillance
What personal data is collected, how it is used, and users' rights regarding their data
Security features that protect users from hackers
The editorial standards used to ensure content accuracy
quizQuestions.explanation: Privacy policies (required in many jurisdictions) describe what data a company collects, how it uses and shares that data, and what rights users have regarding their personal information.

quizQuestions.question: End-to-end encryption in messaging apps means:
options: Messages are stored permanently on company servers after delivery
Messages can only be read by the sender and intended recipient, not the company or third parties
The app reads all messages to improve its recommendation algorithm
Messages are automatically deleted after 24 hours
quizQuestions.explanation: End-to-end encryption ensures that only the sender and recipient can read a message; even the company providing the service cannot access the message content.

quizQuestions.question: The digital footprint you leave when using apps and websites includes:
options: Only information you deliberately post publicly
All data generated by your activity including location, clicks, purchases, and browsing history
Only information you share directly in messages to other users
Only photos and videos you upload to social media platforms
quizQuestions.explanation: Your digital footprint encompasses all data trails — including passive data like location, browsing history, click behavior, device type, and purchase patterns — generated by your online activity.

quizQuestions.question: The primary difference between misinformation and disinformation is:
options: Disinformation spreads faster than misinformation
Disinformation is deliberately false and intended to deceive; misinformation is false but spread without malicious intent
Misinformation only exists on social media while disinformation appears in traditional news
Disinformation only involves images and video; misinformation is textual
quizQuestions.explanation: The key distinction is intent: disinformation is intentionally false and created to deceive, while misinformation is false information shared by people who genuinely (but wrongly) believe it is true.

quizQuestions.question: Misinformation spreads rapidly on social media primarily because:
options: Social media platforms fact-check content too slowly to prevent spread
Emotionally engaging content, including false claims, is shared faster than dry, accurate information
Most social media users intentionally share false information
Algorithms are programmed to prioritize misinformation
quizQuestions.explanation: Research (MIT Media Lab) shows false news spreads faster than true news on social media because false stories tend to be more novel and emotionally arousing, driving higher engagement.

quizQuestions.question: A deepfake in digital media refers to:
options: A satirical news article clearly labeled as fiction
AI-generated synthetic video or audio that realistically depicts a person saying or doing things they never did
An anonymous social media account used to spread false information
A misleading headline that contradicts the article's actual content
quizQuestions.explanation: Deepfakes are AI-generated synthetic media that realistically simulate a person's appearance, voice, or actions — creating videos of people saying or doing things they never actually did.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the most effective individual strategy for avoiding sharing misinformation?
options: Only sharing content from accounts with large follower counts
Pausing, verifying the source, and searching for corroborating evidence before sharing
Trusting content shared by personal friends and family
Only sharing content that includes images or video evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: The most effective individual strategy is to pause before sharing, identify the original source, and search for independent corroboration from credible outlets before amplifying any claim.

quizQuestions.question: Propaganda differs from journalism primarily in that propaganda:
options: Is produced by government organizations rather than private companies
Deliberately manipulates emotions and information to advance a particular political agenda
Is always false, while journalism is always true
Is broadcast on television rather than published in newspapers
quizQuestions.explanation: Propaganda uses selective information, emotional manipulation, and misleading framing to advance a specific political agenda, unlike journalism which aims for factual accuracy and fairness.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of a 'free press' as the 'fourth estate' means:
options: The press is the fourth largest employer in the economy
The press serves as an independent watchdog on the other three branches of power
The press is the fourth branch of government with formal legal authority
The press has the right to collect taxes to fund its operations
quizQuestions.explanation: The fourth estate concept portrays a free press as an independent institution that monitors and holds accountable the government, judiciary, and other powerful institutions on behalf of citizens.

quizQuestions.question: Prior restraint in press law refers to:
options: Journalists restraining themselves from publishing embarrassing information
Government preventing publication of material before it is published
Courts ordering media to correct inaccurate reporting
Publishers reviewing content before it goes to the printing press
quizQuestions.explanation: Prior restraint is government censorship that prevents publication before it occurs — the most serious First Amendment violation, as established in Near v. Minnesota (1931) and Pentagon Papers (1971).

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the greatest CURRENT threat to press freedom in the United States?
options: The government directly censoring which stories can be published
Financial pressures causing local newsroom closures and reduced investigative capacity
Technology making it impossible to publish print newspapers
International organizations restricting American journalists from reporting abroad
quizQuestions.explanation: The most significant current threat to press freedom is economic — declining advertising revenue and corporate consolidation have led to widespread local newsroom closures, reducing investigative reporting capacity.

quizQuestions.question: 'News deserts' refers to communities that:
options: Receive only international news without local coverage
Have lost all or most local news coverage due to newsroom closures
Have extremely hot and dry climates that limit media infrastructure
Prefer entertainment media over news content
quizQuestions.explanation: News deserts are geographic areas — often rural or low-income — where local newspapers and broadcasters have closed, leaving communities without consistent coverage of local government, schools, and issues.

quizQuestions.question: A free press is considered essential to democracy because it:
options: Ensures all government officials are caught and prosecuted for corruption
Provides citizens with information necessary for informed participation and holds power accountable
Guarantees that elections are decided on factual information
Makes all government documents freely available to any citizen
quizQuestions.explanation: A free press enables citizens to make informed decisions, monitors government and institutional power, exposes corruption and abuse, and facilitates the public discourse essential to democratic self-governance.

quizQuestions.question: Artificial intelligence in media is most significantly disrupting which area of journalism?
options: Investigative reporting requiring document analysis and interviews
Automated generation of formulaic news articles (sports scores, financial reports, weather)
Photography and video production for broadcast news
Editorial decision-making at major national newspapers
quizQuestions.explanation: AI is most widely used in journalism to automatically generate routine, data-driven articles (earnings reports, weather, sports statistics) at high speed and low cost.

quizQuestions.question: Virtual reality (VR) journalism aims to create 'presence' in storytelling by:
options: Using celebrity journalists to increase viewer engagement
Immersing the viewer in a 360-degree simulated environment to experience events firsthand
Broadcasting live news events faster than traditional networks
Using animated characters instead of real journalists to present news
quizQuestions.explanation: VR journalism places viewers in a fully immersive environment (e.g., a refugee camp, a war zone), using the sense of 'presence' to build empathy and understanding in ways flat screens cannot.

quizQuestions.question: The rise of the creator economy has most significantly changed media by:
options: Eliminating all forms of traditional media broadcasting
Enabling individuals to produce and distribute media content directly to audiences without institutional intermediaries
Creating a new government regulatory framework for media production
Reducing the total amount of media content available to consumers
quizQuestions.explanation: The creator economy enables individual content creators (YouTubers, podcasters, writers) to bypass traditional media gatekeepers and directly reach audiences through digital platforms.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a key ethical concern about AI-generated media content?
options: AI content is always more accurate than human-produced content
AI-generated deepfakes and synthetic content can be used to deceive and manipulate audiences at scale
AI journalism is more biased than human journalism
AI systems cannot generate creative or original content
quizQuestions.explanation: AI-generated content — especially deepfakes and synthetic text — can produce realistic but completely false information at unprecedented scale, posing serious risks for misinformation and manipulation.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes a responsible media consumer in an increasingly AI-driven media landscape?
options: Trusting only AI-generated content because AI is more objective than humans
Developing skills to identify AI-generated content and applying critical evaluation to all media regardless of source
Avoiding all digital media to escape AI influence
Relying entirely on social network recommendations for trustworthy content
quizQuestions.explanation: Responsible media consumption in an AI era requires developing skills to identify synthetic content, apply critical evaluation frameworks to all sources, and maintain skepticism even of seemingly realistic content.

questions.question: Harold Lasswell identified which three functions of mass media?
options: Entertainment, advertising, and propaganda
Surveillance, correlation, and cultural transmission
Gatekeeping, framing, and agenda-setting
Production, distribution, and consumption
questions.explanation: Lasswell identified surveillance (monitoring the environment), correlation (interpreting events), and cultural transmission (passing on social norms and values) as core media functions.

questions.question: Media literacy involves all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Analyzing who created a media message
Evaluating what techniques are used to attract attention
Accepting all media messages from established outlets without question
Identifying what values or viewpoints are represented or absent
questions.explanation: Media literacy requires critical evaluation of ALL media messages, including those from established outlets — uncritical acceptance is the opposite of media literacy.

questions.question: According to agenda-setting theory, media's most powerful influence is on:
options: What audiences think about specific issues
Which issues audiences consider important
How audiences vote in elections
What products audiences purchase
questions.explanation: Agenda-setting proposes that media influences issue salience — which topics the public considers most important — rather than directly determining specific opinions or behaviors.

questions.question: Cultivation Theory's 'mean world syndrome' predicts that heavy TV viewers will:
options: Become more socially connected through shared viewing experiences
Overestimate the danger and violence in the real world
Become desensitized to violence and feel less empathy
Be more likely to commit violent crimes themselves
questions.explanation: Mean world syndrome describes the tendency of heavy TV viewers to perceive the real world as more dangerous than it is, based on heavy exposure to crime and violence in TV content.

questions.question: In Bandura's Social Learning Theory, 'modeling' refers to:
options: Creating fashion content for social media
Imitating behaviors observed in others, including media figures
Designing research studies on media effects
Formally teaching behaviors through structured instruction
questions.explanation: Modeling is the process of observing and imitating behaviors displayed by others — a key mechanism through which media can influence audience attitudes and behaviors.

questions.question: The framing of the same immigration story as either a 'crisis' or an 'opportunity' demonstrates:
options: Balanced journalism presenting both sides of an issue
How word and image choices shape audience interpretation of events
The agenda-setting power to determine which issues are covered
The objectivity required by professional journalism standards
questions.explanation: Framing the same event with different language, images, and emphasis shapes how audiences interpret and emotionally respond to the issue — a demonstration of framing theory.

questions.question: Media's role in consumer culture is primarily to:
options: Educate consumers about product safety and quality
Create and maintain desire for goods through advertising that links products to aspirational identities
Provide objective comparisons between competing products
Protect consumers from deceptive business practices
questions.explanation: Media's primary role in consumer culture is advertising — using emotional appeals, social comparison, and aspirational imagery to create and sustain desire for consumer goods.

questions.question: The Bechdel Test evaluates gender representation in film by checking whether the film:
options: Has a female director and female lead actor
Features two named women who talk to each other about something other than a man
Shows women in non-stereotypical professional roles
Has an equal ratio of male to female speaking roles
questions.explanation: The Bechdel Test applies three criteria: at least two women, who are named, who talk to each other, about something other than a man — a minimal standard many films still fail.

questions.question: Symbolic annihilation in media, as described by Gaye Tuchman, refers to:
options: Violence against symbolic characters in video games
The rendering of certain groups invisible through absence or trivialization in media
Censoring historically offensive media content
The decline of symbolic storytelling in modern media
questions.explanation: Symbolic annihilation describes how groups are effectively made invisible or insignificant in media through underrepresentation, stereotyping, or trivializing their portrayals.

questions.question: Confirmation bias in media consumption describes the tendency to:
options: Confirm whether media sources have been fact-checked before trusting them
Seek out information that reinforces existing beliefs and reject contradicting information
Read multiple sources before forming any opinion
Confirm the identity of journalists before trusting their reporting
questions.explanation: Confirmation bias leads people to favor media content that confirms their pre-existing beliefs and to discount, avoid, or dismiss information that challenges those beliefs.

questions.question: Horse race journalism in election coverage prioritizes:
options: Policy analysis and candidate qualifications
Poll standings, momentum, and competition rather than substantive policy differences
Investigation of campaign finance and donor influence
Equal coverage of all registered candidates
questions.explanation: Horse race journalism focuses on who is ahead in polls and the competitive dynamics of campaigns rather than the substantive policy positions and qualifications of candidates.

questions.question: The 'hypodermic needle' model of media effects proposed that media messages:
options: Have gradual, cumulative effects that build up over time
Directly and powerfully inject attitudes into passive, uniform audiences
Have different effects depending on individual audience characteristics
Are filtered through social groups before influencing individuals
questions.explanation: The hypodermic needle (magic bullet) model was an early, now discredited model proposing that media messages directly determine audience attitudes and behaviors uniformly.

questions.question: Research on social media and adolescent mental health has most consistently found:
options: Social media significantly improves teen mental health through peer connection
Heavy social media use is associated with increased rates of depression and anxiety, especially among girls
Social media has no measurable effect on teen mental health
The effects of social media on mental health are identical for all adolescents
questions.explanation: Research by Twenge, Haidt, and others has found correlations between heavy social media use and increased depression and anxiety, particularly among adolescent girls.

questions.question: Surveillance capitalism (Zuboff) refers to an economic system where:
options: Governments monitor citizens for national security purposes using media
Tech companies extract and monetize user behavioral data as their primary product
Consumers pay companies to monitor their own online safety
Journalists surveil corporate and government behavior on behalf of citizens
questions.explanation: Surveillance capitalism describes how tech companies like Google and Facebook accumulate behavioral data from users and sell predictions about future behavior to advertisers.

questions.question: The press's watchdog function in democracy is most directly threatened by:
options: Too many journalists working in investigative reporting
Financial pressures that cause newsrooms to close or cut investigative staff
Government prohibiting journalists from publishing news at all
Audiences spending too much time reading news
questions.explanation: The economic challenges facing journalism — advertising revenue loss, corporate consolidation, newsroom closures — are the most significant current threats to the press's ability to perform its democratic watchdog function.

questions.question: Second-level agenda-setting (framing) goes beyond first-level agenda-setting by proposing that media also influences:
options: How much time audiences spend thinking about issues
Which attributes and perspectives audiences associate with a given issue
Whether audiences vote in elections
Which specific news outlets audiences trust most
questions.explanation: Second-level agenda setting proposes that media not only determines WHAT audiences think about (first level) but also shapes HOW they think about those issues through the attributes emphasized.

questions.question: Cultivation Theory predicts that media effects are strongest for which type of audience?
options: Light viewers who watch TV occasionally
Heavy, long-term viewers who consume large amounts of similar content
Young children who watch educational programming
Highly educated adults who consume diverse media
questions.explanation: Cultivation Theory predicts that effects accumulate through heavy, long-term, consistent exposure — making heavy television viewers most susceptible to cultivated worldview beliefs.

questions.question: For social learning from media to lead to behavior change, Bandura's model requires all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Attention to the modeled behavior
Retention (memory) of the behavior
Direct personal experience with consequences of the behavior
Motivation to reproduce the behavior
questions.explanation: Social learning can occur without direct personal experience with consequences — observing a model being rewarded or punished is sufficient motivation for learning.

questions.question: Media filter bubbles in political contexts are most likely to lead to:
options: Greater understanding of opposing political viewpoints
Increased political polarization as people receive only perspective-confirming information
More accurate political information as algorithms remove false content
Greater civic engagement through shared media experiences
questions.explanation: Filter bubbles expose people primarily to content that confirms existing political beliefs, potentially reinforcing extreme positions and reducing exposure to compromise or alternative perspectives.

questions.question: The 'whitewashing' problem in Hollywood refers to:
options: The use of bright lighting that makes all actors appear lighter on screen
Casting White actors in roles originally written for or historically belonging to people of color
The historical exclusion of color from film until Technicolor was developed
The practice of colorizing classic black-and-white films
questions.explanation: Whitewashing is the ongoing practice of replacing characters of color with White actors in adaptations, remakes, or original productions — reducing non-White representation in mainstream media.

questions.question: Research linking social media use to body dissatisfaction primarily involves the mechanism of:
options: Direct media instructions telling users how to feel about their bodies
Upward social comparison with idealized images encountered on social media platforms
Cyberbullying and direct peer criticism of body types
Social media algorithms promoting eating disorder content
questions.explanation: Social comparison theory explains that exposure to idealized body images on social media triggers upward social comparisons — comparing oneself unfavorably to these unrealistic standards.

questions.question: Disinformation campaigns on social media are most effective because they:
options: Use complex arguments that are difficult for ordinary people to evaluate
Exploit emotional resonance, confirmation bias, and algorithmic amplification to spread rapidly
Are backed by scientific data that appears credible
Can only be spread by government-sponsored accounts with large followings
questions.explanation: Disinformation exploits emotional triggers and confirmation bias, and is amplified by algorithms optimizing for engagement — making it spread faster and wider than factual corrections.

questions.question: News deserts harm democracy primarily because they:
options: Force citizens to rely exclusively on entertainment for information
Leave communities without local accountability journalism to monitor government, schools, and businesses
Create too much competition among the remaining news outlets
Encourage citizens to trust social media as their primary news source
questions.explanation: When local newsrooms close, communities lose coverage of local government, school boards, and businesses — creating information vacuums that enable corruption and reduce civic accountability.

questions.question: AI-generated deepfakes pose the most serious risk to:
options: The financial profitability of the film industry
Trust in visual and audio evidence, as any video could potentially be fabricated
The employment of human actors in entertainment
International copyright and intellectual property law
questions.explanation: Deepfakes undermine the foundational epistemic role of video and audio evidence — when any realistic video can be fabricated, the ability to trust visual documentation of events is fundamentally threatened.

questions.question: The creator economy has democratized media production by:
options: Eliminating the need for any quality standards in media content
Enabling individuals to produce and distribute content directly to global audiences without institutional gatekeepers
Creating new government regulations that make media production easier
Reducing the total number of media sources available to audiences
questions.explanation: The creator economy empowers individuals to bypass traditional media gatekeepers (studios, publishers, networks) and directly reach audiences through YouTube, Substack, podcasts, and social platforms.

questions.question: The concept of 'manufactured consent,' developed by Noam Chomsky and Edward Herman, argues that mainstream media:
options: Deliberately falsifies news to entertain audiences
Serves elite interests by framing issues within boundaries acceptable to corporate and government power
Is controlled by a secret international organization
Intentionally creates political division to increase audience engagement
questions.explanation: Chomsky and Herman's propaganda model argues that corporate-owned mainstream media systematically frames news within boundaries that serve the interests of corporate and political elites.

questions.question: End-to-end encryption protects users primarily by:
options: Preventing them from receiving spam and unsolicited messages
Ensuring only sender and recipient can read message content — not companies or governments
Deleting messages after they have been read
Verifying the identity of all parties in a conversation
questions.explanation: End-to-end encryption mathematically ensures that message content can only be decrypted by the sender and intended recipient, making it unreadable even to the platform provider.

questions.question: Prior restraint — government preventing publication before it occurs — is most clearly prohibited by:
options: The Fourth Amendment to the US Constitution
The First Amendment's protection of press freedom
FCC licensing regulations for broadcast media
Copyright law's protections for creative works
questions.explanation: The First Amendment has been interpreted by the Supreme Court (Near v. Minnesota, Pentagon Papers) to establish a very strong presumption against prior restraint of the press.

questions.question: Lifestyle advertising is most effective at selling because it:
options: Provides detailed information about product features and price
Associates products with aspirational identities, social groups, and emotional states
Uses scientific endorsements from independent research institutions
Compares the product favorably to all competing alternatives
questions.explanation: Lifestyle advertising sells not just a product but a desired identity — making consumers feel that purchasing the product will bring them closer to the social group, status, or emotional state depicted.

questions.question: Racial stereotyping in media is most harmful when:
options: It appears only in historical dramas set in the past
It is repeated consistently across many media platforms, normalizing and reinforcing biased perceptions
It is challenged by other characters within the same program
It is clearly labeled as fiction and not based on real people
questions.explanation: The harmful effects of racial stereotyping are cumulative — consistent repetition across multiple media normalizes biased perceptions, shaping both audience attitudes toward stereotyped groups and how those groups see themselves.

questions.question: FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) as a social media effect refers to:
options: Anxiety about losing access to social media accounts
Anxiety caused by social comparison with curated images of peers' social lives
Fear of being left behind technologically as new platforms emerge
Concern about missing breaking news by not checking social media constantly
questions.explanation: FOMO is the anxiety driven by social comparison — seeing others' seemingly exciting, socially rich lives on social media creates feelings of inadequacy and exclusion.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between media and democracy?
options: Democracy requires that all media be owned by the government
A free, independent press is essential to democracy by informing citizens and holding power accountable
Democracy functions best with minimal media involvement in political affairs
Media should only report facts without analysis to preserve democratic neutrality
questions.explanation: Democratic theory holds that informed citizens require access to accurate information and that a free press is essential for monitoring government, exposing corruption, and facilitating public discourse.

questions.question: The difference between objective reporting and advocacy journalism is:
options: Objective reporting is always more accurate than advocacy journalism
Objective journalism aims for neutrality while advocacy journalism openly promotes a particular perspective or cause
Advocacy journalism is always funded by political organizations
Only objective journalism is protected by the First Amendment
questions.explanation: Objective journalism strives for impartiality and neutrality, while advocacy journalism openly identifies a viewpoint and works to advance it — both are legal under the First Amendment but differ in editorial philosophy.

questions.question: Intersectionality as a framework for analyzing media representation considers how:
options: Multiple media platforms intersect to create a unified media ecosystem
Race, gender, class, and other identities overlap to create distinct representation experiences
Film, TV, and digital media intersect in the contemporary media landscape
International and domestic media create intersecting cultural influences
questions.explanation: Intersectionality, developed by Kimberlé Crenshaw, examines how overlapping social identities (race, gender, class, sexuality) interact to shape distinct experiences of representation, privilege, and discrimination in media.

questions.question: Which of the following best exemplifies responsible digital citizenship in an age of misinformation?
options: Sharing information quickly so important truths spread faster than falsehoods
Pausing before sharing, verifying through credible sources, and only sharing what you can confirm
Trusting only government sources as definitively accurate
Avoiding all digital media to prevent exposure to false information
questions.explanation: Responsible digital citizenship requires verification habits — pausing, checking sources, and confirming claims through credible outlets — before adding one's voice to the spread of any information.
